lessons.title: What is a computer network?
lessons.objectives:
I can define a computer network
I can give an advantage and a disadvantage of a computer network
I can describe how a computer behaves in a peer-to-peer network
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: network · graph · node · edge · computer network · client · server · peer-to-peer. TA: hand out both worksheets; read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — networks are everywhere (friends messaging, a train map). Pupils CHOOSE on the starter sheet; no writing. This is lesson 1 so there is no recap.
2. (8–22) I-do → we-do. Teacher defines a computer network as “two or more computers connected and sharing” (I do); class does the “is it a network?” card-sort and the node/edge graph questions (we do). Embed the network-of-people image. Likely error: calling one computer on its own a network — fix-words “connected AND sharing.”
3. (22–42) You-do — tick-all the good points of a network, then term↔definition matching (Support), advantage + disadvantage in writing (Core), and ORDER the steps of a peer-to-peer file request (Challenge).
   • Support: keyword↔definition matching.  • Core: one advantage + one disadvantage; what peer-to-peer means.  • Challenge: order the four P2P steps and explain “equal responsibility.” TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
4. (42–50) Consolidate — the one thing they would most want to share written + photo of finished sheet (show-your-work).
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three “I can…”; each pupil names one thing a network lets you share.
Differentiation is on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: The client–server model
lessons.objectives:
I can describe the role of a client and a server
I can compare client–server with peer-to-peer
I can describe a PAN, a LAN and a WAN
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: client–server · client · server · peer-to-peer · PAN · LAN · WAN. TA: hand out both worksheets; read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — RECAP last lesson (define a computer network; peer-to-peer = every computer equal). Pupils CHOOSE on the starter sheet.
2. (8–22) I-do → we-do. Teacher defines client (asks) and server (answers) (I do); class sorts the “client / server” jobs (we do). Embed the server icon. Likely error: swapping client and server — fix-words “client asks, server answers.”
3. (22–42) You-do — card-sort PAN/LAN/WAN examples, then differentiate.
   • Support: term↔definition matching (client/server/peer-to-peer/PAN/LAN/WAN).  • Core: choose the right model for two scenarios (single-correct) + one disadvantage of client–server.  • Challenge: tick-all the disadvantages of client–server and explain “single point of failure.” TA: prompt, do not supply the answer.
4. (42–50) Consolidate — is our school network a PAN, LAN or WAN, with a reason + photo of finished sheet.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three “I can…”; each pupil names one job a server does.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: A server icon.png
resources.sourceAttribution: Cisco networking icon, supplied with the Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation.

lessons.title: Network hardware
lessons.objectives:
I can describe what each piece of network hardware does
I can define a MAC address
I can choose the right hardware for a network
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: wireless access point (WAP) · router · switch · hub · NIC · bridge · MAC address. TA: hand out both worksheets; read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — RECAP PAN/LAN/WAN. Pupils CHOOSE on the starter sheet.
2. (8–24) I-do → we-do. Teacher introduces each device slowly, one at a time, pausing to check (I do); stress the switch-vs-hub pair (switch = one device, hub = all devices). Embed the router and switch icons. Then define a MAC address as a NIC’s built-in unique name tag. Likely error: confusing MAC with IP — fix-words “MAC is built in and stays.”
3. (24–42) You-do — LABEL the laptop ports (find the network/NIC port) — very low writing, strong visual — then differentiate.
   • Support: device↔job matching (router/switch/hub/NIC/WAP/bridge).  • Core: what a MAC address is; hub-vs-switch behaviour; what a router does.  • Challenge: pick the device for a scenario and explain a hub-vs-switch difference. TA: prompt, do not supply the answer.
4. (42–50) Consolidate — the easiest device to remember + a memory trick + photo of finished sheet.
5. (50–55) Plenary — “Who am I?” — self-tick the three “I can…”; each pupil names what a router does.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Network topologies
lessons.objectives:
I can describe the star, bus, mesh and ring topologies
I can give an advantage and a disadvantage of a topology
I can choose a topology for a scenario
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: topology · node · edge · terminator · star · bus · ring · mesh. TA: hand out both worksheets; read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — RECAP network hardware (router, switch, MAC address). Pupils CHOOSE on the starter sheet.
2. (8–24) I-do → we-do. Teacher defines topology as “the shape” and sketches each one slowly (I do); class meets star/bus then ring/mesh (we do). Embed the star-and-bus topology diagram. Terminator = stops the signal bouncing back. Likely error: thinking the bus terminators are nodes — fix-words “terminators are caps, not computers.”
3. (24–42) You-do — differentiate.
   • Support: topology↔description matching (star/bus/ring/mesh).  • Core: a terminators fill-in-the-blank; choose the topology for a small/cheap scenario; which topology has a single central node.  • Challenge: card-sort star-vs-mesh features and justify a topology for a busy office. TA: prompt with the advantages/disadvantages on the sheet, do not give the answer.
4. (42–50) Consolidate — best topology for a small home + a reason + photo of finished sheet.
5. (50–55) Plenary — “Which topology am I?” — self-tick the three “I can…”; each pupil names the four topologies.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Wired and wireless transmission media
lessons.objectives:
I can define wired and wireless transmission media
I can describe copper, fibre, WiFi and Bluetooth
I can choose the right media for a scenario
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: transmission media · wired · wireless · copper cable · fibre optic · WiFi · Bluetooth. TA: hand out both worksheets; read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — RECAP hardware and topologies (router, WAP, star). Pupils CHOOSE on the starter sheet.
2. (8–22) I-do → we-do. Teacher defines transmission media as “how the data travels” — wired (a cable) vs wireless (through the air) (I do); class does the wired/wireless card-sort (we do). Embed the network-cable photo. All media carry the same 0s and 1s. Likely error: thinking fibre is wireless because it is fast — fix-words “fibre is a cable full of light.”
3. (22–42) You-do — differentiate.
   • Support: media↔description matching (copper/fibre/WiFi/Bluetooth).  • Core: a copper/fibre signals fill-in-the-blank; choose the media for the TV-studio and coffee-shop scenarios (single-correct).  • Challenge: tick-all the advantages of wireless and give one reason a studio chooses wired. TA: prompt, do not supply the answer.
4. (42–50) Consolidate — which media reaches your home devices, and why + photo of finished sheet.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three “I can…”; each pupil names one difference between copper and fibre.
Optional: the Packet Tracer wired/wireless demo videos — motion/sound, the teacher’s choice given the low-arousal default. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Network performance and routing costs
lessons.objectives:
I can describe the factors that affect network performance
I can calculate how long a file takes to send
I can find the lowest-cost route across a network
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: bandwidth · latency · range · number of devices · transmission rate · routing cost. TA: hand out both worksheets; read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — RECAP transmission media (wired = copper/fibre; fibre = light; Bluetooth = short range). Pupils CHOOSE on the starter sheet.
2. (8–22) I-do → we-do. Teacher introduces the four performance factors (I do), using the concert photo for “number of devices share the bandwidth.” Then the transfer-time formula time = bits ÷ bits-per-second, worked slowly (we do). Likely error: dividing speed by size — fix-words “bits on top, speed underneath.” Calculators are fine.
3. (22–44) You-do — differentiate.
   • Support: factor↔definition matching (bandwidth/latency/range/number of devices).  • Core: two transfer-time choices + a fill-in-the-blank calculation.  • Challenge: add the link costs to pick the lowest-cost route, and explain why more devices slow a network. TA: prompt, do not supply the answer.
4. (44–50) Consolidate — the trickiest step and what helped + photo of finished sheet.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three “I can…”; each pupil names one thing that slows a network.
Optional: the Packet Tracer “investigating routes” videos — motion/sound, the teacher’s choice. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: A crowd all using their phones at once.png

lessons.title: What is the internet?
lessons.objectives:
I can describe the internet as a network of networks
I can describe what an IP address and DNS do
I can describe what a web browser does
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: the internet · IP address · DNS · web browser · URL · WWW · HTML. TA: hand out both worksheets; read the three “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — RECAP network performance (bandwidth, lowest-cost route, more devices = slower). Pupils CHOOSE on the starter sheet.
2. (8–24) I-do → we-do. Teacher separates the internet (the network of networks) from the World Wide Web (the pages) (I do); class does the internet/Web card-sort (we do). Embed the world-network image. Brief history: ARPA built a distributed network to survive damage. Likely error: confusing IP with MAC — fix-words “MAC is built in; IP is given on the network.”
3. (24–44) You-do — differentiate.
   • Support: term↔definition matching (internet/IP/DNS/browser/URL/WWW).  • Core: what DNS does; what an IP address is; a “network of ___” fill-in-the-blank.  • Challenge: ORDER the six steps of visiting a website (URL → DNS → IP → request → HTML → build) and explain why each device needs a unique IP. TA: prompt with “what happens first?”, do not give the order.
4. (44–50) Consolidate — the difference between the internet and the Web in their own words + photo of finished sheet.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the three “I can…”; each pupil says what DNS does.
Optional: the Packet Tracer “investigating DNS” video — motion/sound, the teacher’s choice. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: The internet — networks linked around the world.png

lessons.title: Hosting services
lessons.objectives:
I can describe how servers host services across the internet
I can describe what a web server and a client do
I can explain what cloud computing (software as a service) is
I can list advantages and disadvantages of the cloud
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: web server · hosting · client · data centre · cloud computing · software as a service (SaaS). TA: hand out both worksheets; read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — “an internet minute” on the slide; pupils CHOOSE the scale of activity and RECAP last lesson (web browser, DNS). No writing.
2. (8–22) I-do → we-do. Teacher defines web server vs client and a data centre (I do); class sorts “the web server / the client” on the activity sheet (we do). Likely error: swapping server and client — fix-words “server sends, client asks.” Embed the data-centre server photo.
3. (22–40) You-do — card-sort “in the cloud” vs “only on this device”, then tick-all the advantages of the cloud.
   • Support: term↔definition matching (web server / hosting / cloud / data centre).  • Core: name a SaaS app + one disadvantage.  • Challenge: is the cloud good or bad for the environment, with a reason. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
4. (40–50) Consolidate — best cloud-service answer written + photo of finished sheet (show-your-work).
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; each pupil says one thing a web server does.
Differentiation is on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: Servers in a data centre.png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Protocols
lessons.objectives:
I can explain what a protocol is
I can say why networks need agreed standards
I can name what the common protocols are used for
I can put the steps of a sending protocol in order
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: protocol · standard · Ethernet · WiFi · HTTP · HTTPS · FTP · POP · SMTP · IMAP. TA: hand out both worksheets; read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — rules for everyday communication (answering the phone); pupils CHOOSE and RECAP last lesson (web server, cloud).
2. (8–22) I-do → we-do. Teacher defines a protocol as agreed rules and why standards let different makers work together (I do); class sorts protocols into web / email / files / connecting (we do). Likely error: mixing up the email protocols — fix-words “SMTP Sends Mail To.”
3. (22–40) You-do — protocol↔purpose matching (the strong fit), tick-all true statements, then an ORDER task: the steps a sending protocol follows to chunk and reassemble a long message (a gentle lead-in to TCP/IP next lesson).
   • Support: protocol↔purpose matching.  • Core: the extra “S” in HTTPS; POP vs IMAP.  • Challenge: order the sending-protocol steps. TA: prompt, do not supply the answer.
4. (40–50) Consolidate — easiest protocol + memory trick written + photo of finished sheet.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; each pupil names a protocol and its job.
Differentiation on the SAME task. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: An agreed greeting — both sides follow the same rule.png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: The TCP/IP model
lessons.objectives:
I can name the four layers of the TCP/IP model in order
I can label the layer stack
I can match protocols to their layer
I can explain the difference between TCP and UDP
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: TCP/IP model · application layer · transport layer · internet layer · link layer · TCP · UDP. TA: hand out both worksheets; read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — quick questions (what is a protocol, which requests a web page) and RECAP last lesson (SMTP).
2. (8–22) I-do → we-do. Teacher walks down the four layers once, like envelopes inside envelopes (I do); class ORDERS the four layers and LABELS the layer-stack diagram on the worksheet (we do). The clean unlabelled stack image is embedded for the label task.
3. (22–42) You-do — card-sort each protocol into its layer, then fill-in-the-blank TCP = reliable / UDP = unreliable.
   • Support: layer↔role matching.  • Core: the TCP/UDP fill-in-the-blank.  • Challenge: why a video call can use UDP and not resend lost packets. Likely error: thinking UDP is “broken” — fix-words “UDP trades a few lost bits for speed.” TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
4. (42–50) Consolidate — a memory aid for the four layers written + photo of finished sheet.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; each pupil names the four layers in order.
Optional unplugged demo: pass an envelope through four pupils, each adding a label — kept calm and slow. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: The four-layer TCP/IP stack (unlabelled).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Adapted from Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: The OSI model
lessons.objectives:
I can name the seven OSI layers in order
I can label the layer stack
I can match OSI layers to the TCP/IP model
I can say what each layer does
lessons.outline:
Routine first. This is a short, calm lesson. Vocabulary on board: OSI model · application · presentation · session · transport · network · data link · physical · encapsulation. TA: hand out both worksheets; read the “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — RECAP the TCP/IP model (how many layers, which adds IP addresses, TCP is reliable).
2. (8–22) I-do → we-do. Teacher reads the seven layers top to bottom (I do); class ORDERS the seven layers and LABELS the seven-band stack diagram on the worksheet (we do). The clean unlabelled OSI stack image is embedded for the label task.
3. (22–40) You-do — card-sort the seven OSI layers into the four TCP/IP layers, then a multi-select “which TWO are NOT application-layer protocols” (Ethernet + TCP).
   • Support: layer↔job matching.  • Core: which layer handles MAC addresses; what “conceptual” means.  • Challenge: why engineers use the more detailed OSI model to locate a fault. Likely error: thinking OSI replaces TCP/IP — fix-words “OSI is the map; TCP/IP is the road we drive.” TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
4. (40–48) Consolidate — a memory aid for the seven layers + photo of finished sheet.
5. (48–55) Plenary — self-tick the “I can…”; each pupil names a layer and what it does.
Note: OSI appears on only one exam board — teachers of other specs may skip. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: The seven-layer OSI stack (unlabelled).png
resources.sourceAttribution: Adapted from Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Protecting a network
lessons.objectives:
I can say why network security matters
I can name forms of attack on a network
I can sort social engineering from malicious software
I can explain ways to keep a network safe
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: phishing · pharming · social engineering · malicious software · anti-malware · DDoS · shouldering · blagging · brute force · user access rights. Keep the tone calm — about being careful, not scared. TA: hand out both worksheets; read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — what data the school holds about you and why it must be safe; pupils CHOOSE and RECAP last lesson (MAC addresses / link layer, TCP).
2. (8–22) I-do → we-do. Teacher splits attacks into social engineering (tricking people) vs malicious software (harmful code) (I do); class sorts the attacks (we do). Embed the padlock-on-laptop photo. Likely error: filing a DDoS as social engineering — fix-words “DDoS attacks the machine, not the person.”
3. (22–42) You-do — ORDER the four steps of a pharming attack, tick-all the protection methods, then attack↔definition matching.
   • Support: attack↔definition matching.  • Core: what anti-malware does; why user access rights help.  • Challenge: a school rule to make users safer, and why. Optional: the Packet Tracer pharming simulation. TA: prompt, do not supply the answer.
4. (40–50) Consolidate — the safety rule that matters most written + photo of finished sheet.
5. (50–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; each pupil names one way to keep a network safe.
Note: this is a brief overview — the KS4 Cybersecurity unit goes deeper. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: A padlock on a laptop keyboard.png
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Summative assessment
lessons.objectives:
I can describe how servers and the cloud host services
I can name protocols and what they are used for
I can name the layers of the TCP/IP and OSI models
I can name ways a network is attacked and kept safe
lessons.outline:
Routine first, then a calm assessment lesson — no new content. TA: hand out the end-of-unit quiz; read the 4 “I can…” aloud; reassure that every question links to something covered.
1. (0–5) Settle — read the four “I can…” so pupils know what is being checked. Low-stakes, not a high-pressure exam.
2. (5–50) The quiz — pupils work on their own through the folded summative: 15 single-choice questions (one per topic, single-correct) across hosting, protocols, TCP/IP, OSI and security; a multi-select “good ways to stay safe”; a careers text question; and a confidence slider. Read any question aloud on request. Offer movement breaks as normal. The quiz is SHARED (everyone sits the same paper — the differentiation across this unit lives in the per-lesson activity sheets).
3. (50–55) Plenary — pupils rate their confidence on the slider and take a photo of the finished quiz; a few share which topic they feel strongest on.
Closed questions can be self- or teacher-marked using the answer key in the conversion notes. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.
ANSWER KEY (Part 1): 1 stores/sends pages · 2 software & storage via internet · 3 you control where data is stored (NOT an advantage) · 4 set of rules · 5 HTTPS · 6 SMTP · 7 four · 8 application · 9 internet layer · 10 checks/resends packets · 11 fast video & voice calls · 12 seven · 13 data link layer · 14 people · 15 user access rights. Part 2 (Q16): strong password + keep software updated + log out (distractor: sticky-note password).
